persona.name: Yuki Tanabe

occupation.title: COO and Co-founder
occupation.organization: FlowBoard Inc., a 12-person B2B SaaS startup in Shibuya
occupation.description: Handles operations, hiring, investor relations, and customer success while her co-founder focuses on engineering. On any given day she might be pitching to a VC in the morning, onboarding a new enterprise client after lunch, and debugging a billing issue with the dev team by evening. Lives in perpetual context-switching mode.

persona.education: B.A. in Economics from Keio University. Spent one semester abroad at UC Berkeley, which exposed her to Silicon Valley startup culture. Dropped out of a part-time MBA at Globis after deciding the startup needed her full attention.
long_term_goals: Grow FlowBoard to Series A within 18 months
Build a company culture that doesn't burn people out
Eventually become a mentor for other female founders in Japan
persona.style: Energetic and direct — unusual for Japanese business norms, which she's aware of and leans into deliberately. Dresses in monochrome minimalism: black turtleneck, slim pants, white sneakers. Speaks fast, mixes in English business jargon naturally, and is comfortable interrupting to keep meetings on track. On Slack she uses threads religiously and gets frustrated when people post in channels without context. Laughs easily and self-deprecates about her chaotic calendar.

traits: High-energy multitasker who thrives in chaos
Deeply optimistic about technology solving workplace problems
Generous with her time for people she respects
Competitive but collaborative — wants to win with her team

interests: AI tools for meeting productivity and note-taking
Startup ecosystem events and pitch competitions
Yoga and meditation (aspirational — she rarely makes it to class)
Japanese craft gin and natural wine
Design thinking and UX research methodologies
Podcasts about founder journeys (both Japanese and English)
likes: Tools she can set up in under 5 minutes
Products with generous free tiers for startups
People who follow up after meetings without being asked
dislikes: Enterprise sales processes that take 6 months
Tools that require admin permissions she doesn't have time to manage
Meetings where no one takes notes and decisions evaporate
Japanese business culture's default to consensus over speed
Vendors who can't explain pricing clearly
Being talked down to in tech discussions because she's not an engineer
Sleep deprivation, which she complains about but doesn't fix

beliefs: Speed beats perfection in the early stages
The biggest waste in Japanese companies is time lost to unproductive meetings
Great tools should feel invisible — they just make work happen
Diversity in founding teams leads to better products
skills: Rapid vendor evaluation and SaaS procurement
Hiring and team building for early-stage startups

general: Tries every new meeting tool that gets buzz on Twitter/X within the first week
Sends voice memos on Slack instead of typing long messages
Keeps a running Notion doc of 'tools to evaluate' that she adds to daily
Takes meetings while walking when her calendar is overloaded

morning: Wakes at 7:00, checks Slack and email immediately (bad habit she knows)
Quick shower, black coffee from a drip bag
Walks 10 minutes to the co-working space in Shibuya
workday: Meetings from 9:30 to 18:00 with 30-minute lunch at desk
Customer calls, investor updates, internal standups
Squeezes in tool evaluations between meetings
evening: Picks up a bento from the convenience store on the way home
Scrolls through tech Twitter and Product Hunt
Tries to do 10 minutes of stretching before bed
weekend: Saturday morning: sleeps in until 9, then brunch at a Shibuya cafe
Afternoon: catches up on reading or meets a founder friend
Sunday: attempts yoga class, grocery shopping, meal prep (sometimes)
Often ends up working for a few hours on Sunday evening

health.physical: Slim but not fit — rarely exercises beyond walking. Chronic tension in her shoulders and neck from laptop work. Skips meals more often than she should. Her last health check flagged low iron levels.
health.mental: High baseline anxiety that she channels into productivity. Imposter syndrome surfaces during investor meetings. Has considered therapy but hasn't made the appointment. Energized by social interaction but crashes hard on rare days off.

relationships.name: Ryo Matsuda
relationships.description: 30, quiet engineer who balances Yuki's intensity. They argue productively about priorities. He handles the tech; she handles everything else.

relationships.name: Emi Tanabe
relationships.description: 58, retired school teacher in Kobe. Worries that Yuki works too hard and isn't thinking about marriage. They video call weekly.

relationships.name: Saori Inoue
relationships.description: 29, runs a D2C skincare brand. They share startup war stories over dinner monthly and trade tool recommendations.
